service: When CategoryEngine returns null or confidence ≤ 0.5 for a transaction, determine the correct subcategory from Owomi's 3-tier taxonomy — auto-committing when user history supports it, or surfacing a Quick Tag card otherwise.

evals.description: Routine debit with matching user rule — should auto-assign without Quick Tag

expected_output.action: Write groceries to transactions.subcategory_key; set confidence = 0.9; set source = user_rule
expected_output.notes: category_rules match fires in Rule 1 — engine result is discarded. This is the most common path.

evals.description: Engine returned null, no user rule, narration is a bank transfer — should queue Quick Tag, not assign spending category

input.narration: NIP TO ZENITH BANK JOHN DOE

expected_output.action: Queue Quick Tag card with suggested_subcategory = own_account_transfer
expected_output.notes: Rule 2 fires on NIP TO. Never auto-assign a spending subcategory to a transfer narration.

evals.description: Salary credit — should auto-assign to salary_income

expected_output.action: Write salary_income to transactions.subcategory_key; set confidence = 0.85; set source = payroll_pattern
expected_output.notes: Rule 3 fires: SALARY keyword, positive amount. Straight to auto-commit.

evals.description: Engine returned confidence 0.5 — the gap case — should NOT silently commit, should queue Quick Tag
evals.source: Codebase analysis, 2026-05 — this is the primary bug this brain fixes

expected_output.action: Queue Quick Tag card with suggested_subcategory = online_shopping (as suggestion only, not committed)
expected_output.notes: This is the exact bug the brain fixes. Currently the 0.5 confidence guess is saved silently. Brain must queue Quick Tag instead. Engine suggestion is pre-populated on the card as a starting point.

evals.description: Airtime purchase from MTN — should auto-assign to airtime_and_data

expected_output.action: Write airtime_and_data to transactions.subcategory_key; set confidence = 0.85; set source = agent_rule
expected_output.notes: Rule 5 fires: AIRTIME + MTN. This is a high-frequency transaction type for Nigerian users — important to get right.

evals.description: Remita transaction — should auto-assign to government_payment, not generic services

expected_output.action: Write government_payment to transactions.subcategory_key; set confidence = 0.75; set source = agent_rule
expected_output.notes: Rule 4 fires on REMITA. Engine's 'services' guess is discarded — Remita means government/institutional payment.

evals.description: Edge: Salary advance REPAYMENT — negative amount with salary keyword — should NOT auto-assign as income
evals.source: Ifeoluwa Obadiah interview, 2026-05 — real case from early Owomi testing

expected_output.action: Queue Quick Tag card with no pre-populated subcategory
expected_output.notes: Rule 3 ELSE branch: SALARY keyword present but amount is NEGATIVE. This is a repayment, not income. Auto-assigning salary_income here would be wrong and corrupt the user's income tracking.

evals.description: Edge: Amazon transaction — amount determines subcategory, not just vendor name

expected_output.action: Queue Quick Tag card with suggested_subcategory = entertainment (Prime/Kindle) based on amount < ₦3,000
expected_output.notes: Edge case 2 from decision rules. ₦650 is too small to be a purchase — likely Prime Video or Kindle. Engine's 'online_shopping' guess is presented as option but not pre-committed.

evals.description: Edge: Empty narration — should queue Quick Tag with no suggestion, not error
evals.source: Codebase analysis, 2026-05 — blank narrations from certain bank feeds

expected_output.action: Queue Quick Tag card with no pre-populated subcategory and label: 'Unknown transaction — please identify.'
expected_output.notes: Unwritten rule 6: empty narration is a data quality problem, not an error. Guard all pattern checks against len(narration) == 0 to prevent false matches.

evals.description: Edge: Quick Tag queue is full (7+ items) — should backlog the transaction, not add another card

expected_output.action: Set transactions.categorization_source = 'backlogged'; do NOT queue Quick Tag card
expected_output.notes: Unwritten rule 3: queue above 7 causes user abandonment. Backlog the transaction and retry when queue drains below 5. This case tests whether the agent knows to check queue size before adding.

evals.description: Hard: User rule exists for narration pattern, but amount is 10× the typical amount — anomaly should surface to user

expected_output.action: Queue Quick Tag card with suggested_subcategory = groceries and anomaly note: 'This looks larger than usual — is it still Groceries?'
expected_output.notes: The naive answer is to auto-assign via Rule 1 (user rule exists). The correct answer is to flag the anomaly — ₦420,000 at Shoprite is 23× the user's typical grocery spend. Edge case 4 from decision rules. A smart new hire would just apply the user rule and miss the anomaly.

evals.description: Hard: Positive amount with no income marker — unclassified credit — should queue Quick Tag, not auto-assign to income

expected_output.action: Queue Quick Tag card with no pre-populated subcategory and note: 'We couldn't identify this credit — is it income, a refund, or something else?'
expected_output.notes: Rule 7: positive amount, no income marker. Wrong answer: auto-assign to other_income or leave with CategoryEngine's null result. Credits must be confirmed by the user — an auto-assigned income category corrupts the user's income reports if it's actually a refund or a family transfer.

evals.description: Hard: Transfer narration but user has a category_rule for it — user rule should NOT override the transfer guard

input.narration: NIP TO GTB SAVINGS SELF TRANSFER

expected_output.action: Queue Quick Tag card with suggested_subcategory = own_account_transfer (ignore category_rules match for transfer narrations)
expected_output.notes: This is the hardest case. Rule 1 (user rule) fires but Rule 2 (transfer guard) should override it. Why? The user previously tagged this as 'investment' but it's a NIP transfer — they may have mislabeled it historically. The transfer guard exists specifically to prevent corrupted categorizations from propagating. Surface to user for re-confirmation rather than perpetuating a possible error. Wrong answer: auto-assign to investment because the user rule exists.
